THE FIFTH CAUSE OF ACTION DOES NOT SHOW ON ITS FACE THAT IT IS NECESSARILY BARRED BY THE MANAGER'S OR AGENT'S PRIVILEGE. RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces the basis for the tentative ruling. Cross-Complainant Argues and requests to modify the tentative ruling. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS COMPLAINANT EL BETHEL ARMS, INC., EL BETHEL TERRACE, INC.'s Motion To Quash Subpoena Duces Tecum To John Harrison, Esq.

Cross-complainants El Bethel Arms, Inc. and El Bethel Terrace, Inc.'s motion to quash subpoena duces tecum to John Harrison is denied. Because Mr. Harrison is not now litigation counsel for the moving parties and only served briefly in that role in a related case, the heightened standard in Carehouse does not apply. However, even if it did, cross-defendant Christian Church Homes has presented sufficient facts to meet each element of the heightened standard. The record on this motion shows that Mr. Harrison is likely to be able to provide percipient non-privileged information regarding the contracts at issue in this case. Moving parties and Mr. Harrison himself retain all rights to assert any applicable privileges at the depostion and to production of the requested documents.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Gordon Aiavao, CSR#11216, gaiavao@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/HK)

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court modifies its tentative ruling, and orders as follows: As to DEFENDANTS EL BETHEL ARMS, INC. AND EL BETHEL TERRACE, INC.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT, EBA and EBT's demurrers to the first and sixth causes of action are overruled. Their demurrer to the second cause of action alleged by plaintiff WSB is sustained without leave to amend. Their demurrer to the sixth cause of action is sustained with leave to amend, as the sixth cause of action does not adequately allege negligent misrepresentation. If it can be supported by the evidence, Plaintiffs may allege either promissory fraud or negligent misrepresentation. Their demurrer to the fourth cause of action is overruled. The first cause of action adequately alleges that payment was due upon termination of the ORA. Plaintiff WSB is not a party to the ORA and thus lacks standing to sue for its breach.
